questions.question: What are the different states of a Thread in Java? Draw the thread state diagram.
questions.answer: Java threads have 6 states: NEW, RUNNABLE, BLOCKED, WAITING, TIMED_WAITING, TERMINATED. NEW: created but not started. RUNNABLE: executing or ready to execute. BLOCKED: waiting for monitor lock. WAITING: waiting indefinitely for another thread. TIMED_WAITING: waiting for specified time. TERMINATED: completed execution.

questions.question: Explain the difference between start() and run() methods in Thread class.
questions.answer: start() creates a new thread and calls run() in that new thread. run() just executes the code in the current thread like a normal method call. Calling run() directly doesn't achieve concurrency.

questions.question: How do you create a thread in Java? Compare extending Thread vs implementing Runnable vs implementing Callable.
questions.answer: Three ways: 1) Extend Thread class - simple but limits inheritance. 2) Implement Runnable - flexible, can extend other classes. 3) Implement Callable - can return result and throw exceptions. Preferred: Runnable/Callable with ExecutorService.

questions.question: What is the purpose of join() method? Write code to wait for multiple threads.
questions.answer: join() makes current thread wait until the thread on which it's called completes. Useful when you need to wait for threads to finish before proceeding.

questions.question: What happens when you call interrupt() on a thread? How should a thread handle interruption?
questions.answer: interrupt() sets the interrupted flag. If thread is blocked (sleep, wait, join), it throws InterruptedException. Thread should check isInterrupted() or handle InterruptedException properly by restoring interrupt status.

questions.question: Explain the synchronized keyword. What's the difference between synchronized method and synchronized block?
questions.answer: synchronized ensures mutual exclusion. Synchronized method locks on 'this' (instance) or class (static). Synchronized block allows specifying lock object and narrower scope, improving performance.

questions.question: What is the volatile keyword? When should you use it?
questions.answer: volatile ensures visibility across threads - writes are immediately visible to other threads. It prevents caching in CPU registers. Use for flags, status variables. Does NOT provide atomicity for compound operations.

questions.question: What are Atomic classes? Give examples and explain CAS operation.
questions.answer: Atomic classes (AtomicInteger, AtomicLong, AtomicReference) use CAS (Compare-And-Swap) for lock-free thread-safe operations. CAS compares expected value with current, if matches, swaps atomically.

questions.question: Demonstrate the double-checked locking pattern with volatile.
questions.answer: Double-checked locking optimizes singleton creation. First check without lock, then synchronized block, then second check. volatile prevents instruction reordering that could expose partially constructed object.

questions.question: What is the 'happens-before' relationship in Java?
questions.answer: Happens-before defines memory visibility: if action A happens-before B, then A's results are visible to B. Established by: program order, monitor lock (unlock happens-before lock), volatile write (happens-before read), thread start/join.

questions.question: Compare synchronized vs ReentrantLock. When would you choose each?
questions.answer: synchronized: simpler, automatic release, JVM optimized. ReentrantLock: more flexible (tryLock, timed lock, interruptible, multiple conditions), manual release required. Use synchronized for simple cases, ReentrantLock for advanced features.

questions.question: What is false sharing? How does @Contended annotation help?
questions.answer: False sharing occurs when threads modify variables on same cache line, causing cache invalidation. @Contended adds padding to put variable in separate cache line, improving performance in high-contention scenarios.

questions.question: Write a thread-safe counter using AtomicInteger vs synchronized. Compare performance characteristics.
questions.answer: AtomicInteger uses CAS (lock-free), better for low-medium contention. synchronized uses monitors, better for high contention. AtomicInteger avoids context switching overhead.

questions.question: What is the Executor framework? Why prefer it over creating threads manually?
questions.answer: Executor framework separates task submission from execution. Benefits: thread reuse (reduces creation overhead), configurable pool size, task scheduling, lifecycle management, better resource control.

questions.question: Explain ThreadPoolExecutor constructor parameters.
questions.answer: 7 parameters: corePoolSize (min threads), maximumPoolSize (max threads), keepAliveTime (idle timeout), unit (time unit), workQueue (task queue), threadFactory (thread creator), handler (rejection policy).
questions.code_example:
ThreadPoolExecutor executor = new ThreadPoolExecutor(
    5,                          // corePoolSize
    10,                         // maximumPoolSize  
    60L,                        // keepAliveTime
    TimeUnit.SECONDS,           // unit
    new LinkedBlockingQueue<>(100),
    Executors.defaultThreadFactory(),
    new ThreadPoolExecutor.AbortPolicy()
);

questions.question: Compare different ExecutorService types: newFixedThreadPool, newCachedThreadPool, newSingleThreadExecutor, newScheduledThreadPool.
questions.answer: newFixedThreadPool: fixed size, bounded queue. newCachedThreadPool: unlimited, creates new threads as needed (risk of OOM). newSingleThreadExecutor: single thread, sequential execution. newScheduledThreadPool: supports scheduling/delayed tasks.

questions.question: What are the rejection policies in ThreadPoolExecutor? When does rejection occur?
questions.answer: Rejection occurs when queue is full and maxPoolSize threads are busy. Policies: AbortPolicy (throws exception), CallerRunsPolicy (runs in caller thread), DiscardPolicy (silently drops), DiscardOldestPolicy (drops oldest task).

questions.question: How do you properly shutdown an ExecutorService?
questions.answer: Use shutdown() for graceful shutdown (finishes existing tasks, no new tasks). Use shutdownNow() for immediate (tries to stop, returns pending tasks). Always call shutdown() to prevent resource leaks.

questions.question: What is ForkJoinPool? When would you use it?
questions.answer: ForkJoinPool implements work-stealing algorithm for parallel recursive tasks. Best for divide-and-conquer algorithms (sorting, matrix operations). Common pool used by parallel streams.

questions.question: How do you handle exceptions in ExecutorService tasks?
questions.answer: For Runnable: exceptions are swallowed, use UncaughtExceptionHandler. For Callable/submit(): exceptions wrapped in ExecutionException, get() rethrows. Use Future to retrieve exceptions.

questions.question: Write code to execute multiple tasks and get results in completion order using invokeAll vs invokeAny.
questions.answer: invokeAll: submits all tasks, returns List of Futures in submission order. invokeAny: submits all, returns result of first completed task (cancels others).

questions.question: What is ReentrantLock? What does 'reentrant' mean?
questions.answer: ReentrantLock is a mutual exclusion lock with same semantics as synchronized but more flexible. 'Reentrant' means same thread can acquire lock multiple times (count increments), must unlock same number of times.

questions.question: Compare fair vs non-fair locks. What are the trade-offs?
questions.answer: Fair lock: threads acquire in FIFO order (prevents starvation). Non-fair: allows barging (better throughput, risk of starvation). Default is non-fair for performance.

questions.question: What is Condition? How is it different from Object.wait/notify?
questions.answer: Condition provides await/signal like wait/notify but with multiple wait sets per lock. More flexible: multiple conditions, interruptible wait, timed wait. Requires explicit lock.

questions.question: What is ReadWriteLock? When would you use it?
questions.answer: ReadWriteLock allows multiple readers OR one writer. Improves concurrency for read-heavy workloads. ReadLock: shared, multiple holders. WriteLock: exclusive.

questions.question: What is StampedLock? How does it differ from ReadWriteLock?
questions.answer: StampedLock is more performant than ReadWriteLock with optimistic reads. Returns stamp for validation. Three modes: write, read, optimistic-read. No reentrancy, risk of starvation.

questions.question: How do you prevent deadlock when using multiple locks?
questions.answer: Prevention strategies: 1) Acquire locks in consistent order. 2) Use tryLock with timeout. 3) Use single lock when possible. 4) Use higher-level concurrency utilities.

questions.question: Write code using tryLock with timeout. What are the benefits?
questions.answer: tryLock attempts to acquire lock, returns immediately or after timeout. Benefits: avoids indefinite blocking, enables timeout handling, prevents deadlocks.

questions.question: How does ConcurrentHashMap achieve thread-safety? Compare with Hashtable.
questions.answer: CHM uses CAS + synchronized on nodes/bins (Java 8+), segment locking (Java 7). Allows concurrent reads/writes. Hashtable uses single lock, blocks entire map. CHM doesn't allow null keys/values.

questions.question: Explain compute, computeIfAbsent, and computeIfPresent methods in ConcurrentHashMap.
questions.answer: These are atomic operations. compute: calculate new value. computeIfAbsent: compute only if key missing. computeIfPresent: compute only if key exists. All operations are atomic per-key.

questions.question: What is the difference between ConcurrentHashMap and Collections.synchronizedMap?
questions.answer: CHM: fine-grained locking, concurrent access, iterators don't throw ConcurrentModificationException. synchronizedMap: wraps map with single lock, entire map locked per operation, iterators can fail-fast.

questions.question: What is BlockingQueue? Name common implementations and their use cases.
questions.answer: BlockingQueue is thread-safe queue with blocking operations. Implementations: ArrayBlockingQueue (bounded, array), LinkedBlockingQueue (optionally bounded), PriorityBlockingQueue (unbounded, priority), DelayQueue (delayed elements), SynchronousQueue (handoff).

questions.question: Explain SynchronousQueue. When would you use it?
questions.answer: SynchronousQueue has zero capacity - each insert waits for remove and vice versa. Direct handoff between threads. Use for thread pools with direct handoff (CachedThreadPool uses it).

questions.question: How does DelayQueue work? Provide a use case.
questions.answer: DelayQueue holds elements implementing Delayed interface, only allows taking elements whose delay has expired. Use case: scheduled tasks, cache expiration, timeout management.

questions.question: Write a producer-consumer implementation using BlockingQueue.
questions.answer: BlockingQueue provides built-in blocking for producer-consumer pattern. put() blocks when full, take() blocks when empty. Thread-safe, no explicit synchronization needed.

questions.question: What is transfer() in TransferQueue? How is it different from put()?
questions.answer: TransferQueue extends BlockingQueue with transfer() method. transfer() waits until element is consumed (not just queued). put() returns when element is added to queue. SynchronousQueue is a TransferQueue.

questions.question: What is CompletableFuture? How is it better than Future?
questions.answer: CompletableFuture implements Future + CompletionStage. Benefits: can be manually completed, supports callbacks (thenApply, thenAccept), chaining, combining multiple futures, exception handling. More composable than plain Future.

questions.question: Explain thenApply, thenAccept, and thenRun methods.
questions.answer: thenApply: transforms result, returns new value. thenAccept: consumes result, returns void. thenRun: runs after completion, no result access. All are non-blocking callbacks.

questions.question: How do you combine multiple CompletableFutures? Explain allOf, anyOf, thenCombine.
questions.answer: allOf: waits for all futures, returns CompletableFuture<Void>. anyOf: returns first completed result. thenCombine: combines two futures when both complete.

questions.question: How do you handle exceptions in CompletableFuture?
questions.answer: Methods: exceptionally: provide default value. handle: transform result or exception. whenComplete: observe result/exception. errorAt: throw exception.

questions.question: What is the difference between supplyAsync and runAsync?
questions.answer: supplyAsync: supplies result (Supplier<T>), returns CompletableFuture<T>. runAsync: runs action (Runnable), returns CompletableFuture<Void>. Use supplyAsync when you need result, runAsync for side effects.

questions.question: Write code to fetch data from multiple sources in parallel and combine results.
questions.answer: Use supplyAsync for parallel fetching, thenCombine/allOf to combine. Significantly faster than sequential fetching when sources are independent.

questions.question: What is the Java Memory Model (JMM)? Why is it important?
questions.answer: JMM defines how threads interact through memory, specifies visibility guarantees, ordering constraints. Important because: hardware/compiler optimizations can reorder instructions, threads may see stale values without proper synchronization.
questions.code_example:
int x = 0;  // Thread 1: x = 1; Thread 2: may still see x = 0

volatile int x = 0;  // Thread 2 will see x = 1

synchronized(lock) { x = 1; }  // Unlock makes write visible

questions.question: What are the three properties of concurrency: atomicity, visibility, ordering?
questions.answer: Atomicity: operation completes fully or not at all (no partial results). Visibility: changes made by one thread visible to others. Ordering: operations appear in expected order (no reordering visible).

questions.question: Explain instruction reordering. How does it affect multithreaded code?
questions.answer: Compiler/CPU may reorder instructions for optimization if single-threaded semantics preserved. In multithreading, this can cause unexpected behavior. Prevent with volatile, synchronized, or explicit memory barriers.

questions.question: What is the difference between heap and stack in multithreaded context?
questions.answer: Heap: shared memory, all threads can access. Objects live here. Stack: thread-local, each thread has own stack. Local variables, method calls. No synchronization needed for stack-local data.

questions.question: What is thread-local storage? How does ThreadLocal work?
questions.answer: ThreadLocal provides per-thread variables. Each thread has independent copy. Useful for thread-specific state (user context, transaction). Implemented as Map<Thread, Object> internally.

questions.question: What memory leak issues can occur with ThreadLocal in thread pools?
questions.answer: ThreadLocal entries hold strong references to values. In thread pools, threads live long, so values persist even after use. Must call remove() to prevent memory leaks, especially with ClassLoader references.

questions.question: Explain the safe publication pattern. How do you safely publish objects?
questions.answer: Safe publication ensures object is fully constructed before other threads see it. Patterns: static initializer, volatile field, AtomicReference, final field, lock-protected field, concurrent collection.

questions.question: What are the differences between synchronized, volatile, and atomic variables in terms of JMM guarantees?
questions.answer: synchronized: provides atomicity + visibility + ordering (full lock semantics). volatile: provides visibility + ordering, NOT atomicity. Atomic: provides atomicity + visibility (uses volatile internally + CAS).
